questions.question: Explain the difference between %d and %i format specifiers in printf().
keyPoints: Both %d and %i print signed decimal integers
In printf(), they behave identically - both print decimal representation
In scanf(), %i can interpret octal (0) and hexadecimal (0x) prefixes, but %d only reads decimal

questions.question: What is the purpose of escape sequences in printf()? Give two examples.
keyPoints: Escape sequences allow printing special characters that cannot be typed directly
\n - moves cursor to next line (newline)
\t - inserts horizontal tab space
Other examples: \r (carriage return), \\ (backslash), \" (double quote), %% (percent sign)

questions.question: Explain field width and precision in printf() with an example each.
keyPoints: Field width specifies minimum characters to output (e.g., %5d displays 42 as ' 42')
Precision specifies decimal places for floats (e.g., %.2f shows 3.14159 as 3.14)
For strings, precision limits characters printed (e.g., %.5s shows first 5 characters)
Width can include leading zeros: %05d displays 42 as '00042'

questions.question: What is the return value of printf()? How can it be used?
keyPoints: printf() returns the number of characters successfully printed
Returns negative value if error occurs
Can be used for debugging or validation
Example: int n = printf("Hello"); // n will be 5

questions.question: Write printf() statements to print: (i) Integer (ii) Float with 2 decimals (iii) Character (iv) String
keyPoints: Integer: printf("%d", variable);
Float with 2 decimals: printf("%.2f", variable);
Character: printf("%c", variable);
String: printf("%s", variable);

questions.question: Explain the following format specifiers: %u, %o, %x, %p
keyPoints: %u - prints unsigned decimal integer (non-negative)
%o - prints unsigned octal (base-8) integer
%x - prints unsigned hexadecimal (base-16) using lowercase a-f
%p - prints pointer address in implementation-defined format (usually hexadecimal)

questions.question: What is the output of: printf("%10.3f", 12.3456);
keyPoints: Output will be ' 12.346' (7 spaces + 12.346 = 10 characters)
Width 10 means minimum 10 characters total
Precision .3 means 3 decimal places, so 12.3456 rounds to 12.346
Value is right-aligned by default

questions.question: Explain the complete syntax and working of printf() function in C with examples of various format specifiers, escape sequences, and flags.
keyPoints: printf() is defined in stdio.h, syntax: printf("format", args)
Format string contains literal text, format specifiers (%...), and escape sequences (\...)
Format specifiers: %d/%i(integer), %f(float), %lf(double), %c(char), %s(string), %u(unsigned), %o(octal), %x(hex), %p(pointer)
Escape sequences: \n(newline), \t(tab), \r(carriage return), \" (quote), %%(percent)
Flags: -(left align), +(show +), 0(zero pad), #(alternative form)
Field width and precision: %width.precision specifier
Return value indicates characters printed

questions.question: Predict the output of the following printf statements: (a) printf("%5d", 42); (b) printf("%-5d", 42); (c) printf("%05d", 42); (d) printf("%.2f", 3.14159); (e) printf("%8.2f", 12.5);
keyPoints: (a) ' 42' - right-aligned, 5-char width, space padded
(b) '42 ' - left-aligned within 5-char field
(c) '00042' - right-aligned, 5-char width, zero padded
(d) '3.14' - 2 decimal places, rounded
(e) ' 12.50' - right-aligned, width 8, 2 decimals (shows trailing zero)

questions.question: Write a C program to demonstrate various escape sequences in printf() for formatting output neatly.
keyPoints: Program should include: \n for new line, \t for tab spacing, \\ to print backslash, %% to print percent
Include examples showing formatted tables using tabs
Demonstrate horizontal and vertical alignment
Show practical use in creating structured output like receipts, tables
Code should compile and run successfully demonstrating each escape sequence

questions.question: Explain with examples how to use flags, field width, and precision together in printf() for formatted output.
keyPoints: Flags modify output: -(left align), +(show sign), (space) for positive numbers, # for alternate form, 0 for zero padding
Field width: minimum characters to print, can be combined with flags
Precision: .number specifies decimal places for float, max chars for string, min digits for int
Examples: %-10s (left-align string in 10 chars), %+d (show + for positive), %#x (show 0x prefix), %08.2f (8-char width, 2 decimals, zero-pad)
